heroOverview: Cannabinosis (ICD-10: J66.2) is an occupational lung disease caused by inhaling dust from the hemp plant Cannabis sativa during retting, heckling, or processing, producing airway inflammation and obstruction that closely resembles byssinosis from cotton dust. The condition predominantly affects workers in hemp textile mills and rope-making industries, where airborne fibre and endotoxin concentrations can reach levels sufficient to trigger bronchoconstriction within the first Monday shift after a weekend away from the mill. Worldwide, hemp processing employs millions of workers, particularly in China, Eastern Europe, and Central Asia, where dust control standards vary widely. Acute symptoms — chest tightness, cough, and breathing difficulty — often appear before a full working shift is over and typically ease over the weekend, only to return the following week. With repeated exposures over years, some workers develop persistent airflow limitation indistinguishable from chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. Early recognition and dust reduction can prevent permanent lung damage.

keyStats.prevalence: Prevalence varies by country and dust-control standards; studies in hemp-processing factories in Eastern Europe have found airway symptoms in 10-30% of exposed workers
keyStats.demographics: Predominantly affects hemp textile workers, rope makers, and cannabis agricultural workers; more common where mechanical dust controls are absent
keyStats.avgAge: Symptoms typically appear within the first years of hemp mill employment; chronic disease develops after 10-20 years of exposure
keyStats.globalCases: Exact global burden is unknown; millions of workers worldwide are potentially exposed to hemp dust in countries with active hemp industries

definition: Cannabinosis (ICD-10 J66.2) is a form of byssinosis-spectrum occupational lung disease caused by inhalation of dust generated during the processing of hemp (Cannabis sativa) fibres. The term encompasses acute bronchoconstriction provoked by hemp endotoxin and plant-derived biologic particles, as well as the chronic airflow obstruction that can develop after years of repeated exposure. Mechanistically, inhaled hemp dust — especially the biologically active material released during retting (water-soaking to loosen fibres) — activates toll-like receptors, particularly TLR-4, on airway epithelial cells and macrophages, triggering release of pro-inflammatory cytokines including interleukin-1β, TNF-α, and IL-8. The resulting airway inflammation resembles the response to endotoxin from cotton dust that causes byssinosis (J66.0), and the two conditions share clinical features, epidemiology, and the pathognomonic 'Monday morning' exacerbation pattern. Cannabinosis is classified under ICD-10 J66, 'Airway disease due to specific organic dusts,' alongside byssinosis and flax-dressers' disease. It falls under the subspecialty of occupational pulmonology. The condition is most prevalent in developing and transitional economies where hemp cultivation and processing are expanding but industrial hygiene infrastructure remains limited. Unlike hypersensitivity pneumonitis, cannabinosis does not require a specific sensitisation step — high-dose endotoxin exposure can produce airway effects in any susceptible worker, which is why dose-response relationships with cumulative dust exposure are well documented in the occupational literature.

typesClassification.description: Chest tightness, cough, and dyspnoea occurring on the first working day after a break (classically Monday morning), resolving by the end of the shift or the following day. Spirometry shows reversible obstructive pattern.
Monday symptoms persist and spill into Tuesday or later in the working week. Some permanent spirometric decline is measurable after months to years of continued exposure.
Persistent airflow obstruction that no longer fully resolves during weekends or holidays, clinically indistinguishable from COPD, resulting from years of heavy cumulative hemp dust exposure.
True sensitisation-type occupational asthma triggered by specific hemp allergens, in contrast to the endotoxin-driven acute bronchoconstriction of classical cannabinosis.

primarySymptoms: Chest tightness felt as a band-like constriction across the front of the chest, characteristically worst on the first shift back after a weekend or holiday — the 'Monday chest' pattern that strongly suggests an organic-dust airway disease.
A dry or mildly productive cough beginning within the first hour of entering the hemp processing area and intensifying as the shift progresses.
Shortness of breath on exertion that appears disproportionate to the worker's age and baseline fitness, initially only during heavy tasks but later at rest in severe chronic disease.
Wheezing or a musical wheeze audible when the affected person breathes out, reflecting narrowing of medium and small airways from endotoxin-driven bronchoconstriction.
A sense of airways obstruction or 'breathing through wet cotton' that workers often describe as distinct from the shortness of breath of cardiac disease.
Rhinitis, nasal congestion, and sneezing on entering the hemp mill, reflecting upper airway inflammation from the same inhaled particles.
Fatigue and general malaise during working shifts, sometimes accompanied by low-grade fever in acute high-exposure episodes resembling organic dust toxic syndrome.
Chronic productive cough persisting outside the workplace in workers with Grade II or III disease, reflecting fixed mucous hypersecretion from repeated airway injury.
Progressive exercise intolerance that becomes apparent outside work — workers may notice difficulty climbing stairs or walking uphill that was not present in earlier years.
Sputum production, typically white or grey-tinged, which in chronic cases may increase in volume and become a daily feature similar to chronic bronchitis.
earlyWarningSigns: A transient cough or mild chest tightness on the first morning of the working week that clears by lunchtime.
Slight decrease in measured peak flow between the start and end of a Monday shift.
Nasal irritation or sneezing when entering storage areas where hemp bales are opened.
Recovering 'normal' breathing at the weekend but noticing it takes longer each month before symptoms settle.
emergencySigns: Severe breathlessness at rest with inability to speak in full sentences, indicating acute severe bronchoconstriction requiring emergency bronchodilator treatment.
Oxygen saturation below 92% on a pulse oximeter, signalling respiratory failure that needs urgent hospital assessment.
Cyanosis — blue or grey colouring of the lips or fingernails — suggesting critically low blood oxygen.
Acute high fever above 38.5°C with shaking chills within hours of hemp dust exposure, indicating organic dust toxic syndrome or infectious pneumonia complicating the exposure.
Haemoptysis (coughing up blood), which is not caused by cannabinosis itself and demands immediate investigation to exclude lung cancer, tuberculosis, or bronchiectasis.

causes.description: Gram-negative bacteria colonising retted hemp fibre release lipopolysaccharide endotoxin when dust is aerosolised. Inhaled endotoxin activates TLR-4 receptors on bronchial epithelium and alveolar macrophages, releasing IL-1β, TNF-α, and neutrophil-recruiting chemokines that produce acute airway inflammation and bronchoconstriction within minutes of exposure.

causes.cause: Hemp plant biologic particles and fibres
causes.description: Beyond endotoxin, cannabis-derived proteins, glucans (fungal cell-wall components on mouldy fibres), and fine respirable hemp particles directly irritate the airway lining, contributing to mucus hypersecretion and airway remodelling with repeated exposure over years.

Water or dew retting — soaking hemp stalks to loosen fibres — massively increases microbial colonisation and endotoxin content of the resulting dust. Workers in facilities using retted compared with green (unretted) hemp fibre face substantially higher endotoxin loads and consequently higher rates of acute and chronic disease.

causes.cause: Inadequate dust control in hemp mills
causes.description: Without local exhaust ventilation, wetting of raw material, or enclosed bale-opening systems, respirable dust concentrations in hemp hackling and spinning rooms can reach 5-50 mg/m³, far above the 0.2 mg/m³ inhalable endotoxin levels associated with lung function decline in occupational studies.

Workers with pre-existing asthma, atopy, or bronchial hyperresponsiveness develop symptoms at lower dust concentrations than co-workers, though cannabinosis also occurs in non-atopic individuals because endotoxin produces non-immunological bronchoconstriction via innate immune pathways.

Smoking independently damages mucociliary clearance and increases airway hyperresponsiveness; combined with hemp dust exposure, it accelerates annual FEV1 decline and markedly raises the risk of progressing from acute to chronic disease.

riskFactors.description: Working in hemp textile mills, rope factories, or hemp fibre preparation facilities is the primary occupational risk. Duration and intensity of dust exposure, particularly in hackling and spinning rooms, are the strongest predictors of disease severity.

Workers without fitted P2/P3 respirators or in facilities lacking local exhaust ventilation receive cumulative endotoxin doses many times those associated with lung function decline. Correct respirator use reduces effective exposure by 10-50-fold.

Smokers who work in hemp processing have accelerated FEV1 decline — combining two independent causes of airway inflammation and mucous hypersecretion. Smoking cessation is the single most impactful individual intervention.

Atopic individuals and those with documented asthma or elevated methacholine reactivity develop symptoms at lower dust concentrations and progress to chronic disease more rapidly than non-atopic co-workers.

riskFactors.factor: Long duration of hemp dust exposure
riskFactors.description: Cumulative exposure years are the strongest predictor of progression from reversible Grade I to irreversible Grade III disease. Workers with more than 10-15 years of unprotected exposure face the highest risk of permanent airflow limitation.

Retted hemp carries 5-10 times higher endotoxin loads than green (field-dried) hemp. Facilities processing retted fibre or storing bales in damp conditions report substantially higher symptom prevalence.

Bale opening, hackling, carding, and spinning generate the highest airborne dust concentrations; workers in these stations have higher rates of disease than those in lower-dust roles such as weaving or finishing.

riskFactors.factor: Working in low- and middle-income country facilities without inspection
riskFactors.description: Hemp processing facilities in countries without effective occupational health inspection have documented median airborne dust levels 5-20 times higher than those in regulated Western European mills, explaining the higher disease burden in these settings.

affectedDemographics: Hemp mill workers in hackling, carding, and spinning departments with the highest dust concentrations
Agricultural workers who harvest and rett hemp stalks in the field
Rope and cordage factory workers handling unprocessed hemp fibre
Workers in Eastern Europe, China, Central Asia, and developing nations where hemp is commercially grown and processed
Younger workers who are new to hemp mills — acute symptoms appear quickly, often in the first weeks of employment
Long-tenured hemp workers (10+ years) at risk of chronic irreversible obstruction regardless of smoking status
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of cannabinosis rests on three pillars: a detailed occupational history, a characteristic symptom pattern, and objective lung function testing. A clinician should ask specifically about the type of work performed, the material processed, the presence or absence of ventilation controls, and whether symptoms follow a weekly pattern consistent with the 'Monday morning' phenomenon — chest tightness and cough that are worst at the start of the working week and improve over the weekend. This work-relatedness, known as the circadian-occupational pattern, is pathognomonic for organic-dust airway diseases and should prompt measurement of serial peak expiratory flow rates at work and at home over two to four weeks. A fall in peak flow of 20% or more between the start and end of a shift, or between working and non-working days, strongly supports the diagnosis. Spirometry before and after a shift, and before and after a period away from work, documents the degree of airflow obstruction and its reversibility. In early disease, spirometry may be normal when measured away from work — shift spirometry (before and after a full shift) is therefore essential for detecting Grade I disease. A chest X-ray is often normal or shows only mild hyperinflation; high-resolution CT of the chest may show air trapping, centrilobular emphysema, and bronchial wall thickening in more advanced cases. Bronchial challenge testing with methacholine or specific hemp dust extract can confirm airway hyperresponsiveness but is not routinely required if occupational history and serial spirometry are diagnostic. Bronchoalveolar lavage in atypical cases shows neutrophilia consistent with endotoxin-driven inflammation. The key differential diagnoses are asthma unrelated to work, chronic bronchitis from smoking, hypersensitivity pneumonitis from hemp or mould, and COPD; the work-relatedness of symptoms and the absence of a six-hour delayed febrile reaction (which would suggest hypersensitivity pneumonitis) help distinguish cannabinosis from these conditions.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects work-related airflow obstruction by comparing peak flow at work versus at home over several weeks, with a ≥20% fall on working days supporting an occupational airway diagnosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You use a hand-held peak flow meter at fixed times throughout the day for two to four weeks, recording readings at work and at home. Results are charted to show any work-related pattern.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures the acute fall in FEV1 across a working shift; a shift decrement of ≥5-10% indicates significant acute bronchoconstriction from dust exposure.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You perform a full spirometry test first thing in the morning before starting work, and again at the end of the same shift; the two sets of results are compared.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Establishes the pattern (obstructive, mixed, or normal) and whether any obstruction reverses after a short-acting bronchodilator — reversibility suggests coexisting asthma or early disease.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You take a deep breath and blow as hard and fast as possible into a spirometer; the test is repeated after an inhaled bronchodilator. The whole appointment takes about 30 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies structural changes such as air trapping, centrilobular emphysema, and bronchial wall thickening in workers with advanced disease or when spirometry results are inconsistent with symptoms.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You lie still in a CT scanner for about five minutes while thin cross-sectional images of the lungs are taken; no contrast injection is needed for most cases.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes alternative diagnoses such as pneumonia, pleural effusion, fibrosis, or malignancy, and assesses for hyperinflation in more advanced cases.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standing chest X-ray takes about two minutes; you hold your breath briefly while the image is captured.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures actual airborne hemp dust and endotoxin concentrations at the worker's station to quantify exposure, guide engineering controls, and satisfy workers' compensation requirements.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: An occupational hygienist visits the workplace and places personal sampling pumps on you during a normal shift; filters are sent for gravimetric dust analysis and limulus amebocyte lysate endotoxin assay.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms airway hyperresponsiveness in workers whose resting spirometry is normal; a PC20 below 8 mg/mL defines clinically significant hyperresponsiveness.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You inhale increasing doses of methacholine aerosol separated by spirometry measurements until FEV1 falls 20% or the maximum dose is reached; the test takes about one hour and is stopped as soon as the threshold is met.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of cannabinosis follows a two-track strategy: remove or reduce the exposure that is driving airway injury, and manage the resulting airway disease with conventional medications. The most important intervention at any stage is dust control — either by engineering controls at the workplace (local exhaust ventilation, enclosed bale-opening, wetting of raw fibre, general dilution ventilation) or, when these are insufficient, by fitting the worker with an appropriate respiratory protective device. Where the exposure cannot be adequately controlled and the worker has Grade II or III disease, redeployment to a dust-free role or leaving hemp processing entirely must be discussed, weighing the medical risk of continued exposure against the socioeconomic consequences. For acute bronchoconstriction, a short-acting beta-2 agonist (SABA) such as salbutamol used before shifts provides symptomatic relief but does not alter disease progression and should not substitute for exposure reduction. Workers with Grade II or III disease who have persistent airflow obstruction receive the same long-acting bronchodilator regimen used in COPD — long-acting muscarinic antagonists (LAMA) and long-acting beta-2 agonists (LABA) — because the pathophysiological end-state is essentially the same as tobacco-related COPD. Inhaled corticosteroids are added if the patient has features of coexisting asthma or significant eosinophilic airway inflammation. Smoking cessation is mandatory and dramatically slows progression in smokers. Workers with documented sensitisation-type occupational asthma from hemp allergens (distinct from non-immunological cannabinosis) may benefit from immunotherapy, though evidence in hemp specifically is limited. Pulmonary rehabilitation is recommended for workers with Grade III disease and FEV1 below 50% predicted. Annual influenza and pneumococcal vaccination reduces the risk of infective exacerbations. Workers' compensation and occupational disease notification should be initiated in all confirmed cases, both to support the individual and to trigger a workplace inspection that may prevent disease in co-workers.

medicalTreatments.name: Short-acting beta-2 agonist (SABA) — salbutamol/albuterol
medicalTreatments.description: Inhaled before entering the hemp mill to prevent or blunt the acute bronchoconstriction of Grade I cannabinosis; also used as rescue medication during symptomatic episodes. Acts within minutes by relaxing bronchial smooth muscle.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Pre-shift SABA use reduces acute shift FEV1 decrement by approximately 50-70% in short-term trials, providing effective acute symptom control, though it does not prevent long-term lung function decline.

medicalTreatments.name: Long-acting muscarinic antagonist (LAMA) — tiotropium or umeclidinium
medicalTreatments.description: Prescribed for workers with persistent Grade II or III airflow obstruction; blocks muscarinic M3 receptors in airway smooth muscle to produce sustained 24-hour bronchodilation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: LAMA therapy improves FEV1 by 100-150 mL and reduces exacerbation frequency by approximately 17-20% compared with placebo in COPD-spectrum disease, the same magnitude expected in chronic cannabinosis.

medicalTreatments.name: Long-acting beta-2 agonist (LABA) — formoterol or salmeterol
medicalTreatments.description: Added to LAMA for workers with moderate-severe persistent obstruction (FEV1 < 60% predicted); dual bronchodilation provides additive benefits over either agent alone.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: LABA/LAMA combination improves FEV1 by 60-80 mL over LAMA alone and further reduces symptom burden scores, as shown in large COPD trials and extrapolated to occupational obstructive disease.

medicalTreatments.name: Inhaled corticosteroid (ICS) added to dual bronchodilator
medicalTreatments.description: Recommended when the worker has features of coexisting asthma — eosinophil counts above 300 cells/µL, significant reversibility, or frequent exacerbations despite dual bronchodilation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Triple therapy (ICS/LABA/LAMA) reduces moderate-to-severe exacerbations by approximately 15-20% over dual bronchodilation in exacerbation-prone COPD, the best available proxy for severe chronic cannabinosis.

medicalTreatments.name: Smoking cessation pharmacotherapy
medicalTreatments.description: Varenicline, bupropion, or nicotine replacement therapy combined with behavioural support; smoking cessation is the single most effective intervention for slowing FEV1 decline in any occupational obstructive disease.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Varenicline achieves 12-month abstinence in approximately 22-25% of motivated smokers, roughly twice the placebo quit rate; the benefit to lung function trajectory is the highest of any intervention in hemp workers who smoke.

medicalTreatments.name: Pulmonary rehabilitation programme
medicalTreatments.description: Structured 6-12 week multidisciplinary programme of supervised exercise training, breathing techniques, education, and psychosocial support for workers with Grade III disease, FEV1 below 50% predicted, or disabling dyspnoea.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Pulmonary rehabilitation improves 6-minute walk distance by a clinically significant 30-50 metres and reduces Hospital Anxiety and Depression scores, with benefits maintained for up to 12 months in COPD-equivalent populations.

alternativeTreatments.name: Engineering dust controls as primary prevention
alternativeTreatments.description: Local exhaust ventilation at bale-opening stations, enclosed processing, wetting of raw hemp during handling, and general dilution ventilation are the most effective interventions — the 'treatment' for cannabinosis at a population level is eliminating the cause rather than medicating the consequences.

alternativeTreatments.name: Respiratory protective equipment
alternativeTreatments.description: Correctly fitted half-face respirators with P3 filters, or powered air-purifying respirators, reduce endotoxin inhalation by 10-100-fold; effective as an adjunct when engineering controls cannot bring dust below action levels, but require fit-testing and worker training.

alternativeTreatments.name: Pre-shift antihistamines (limited evidence)
alternativeTreatments.description: Some workers with significant nasal symptoms use oral antihistamines before shifts; evidence for benefit on lower airway disease is limited and antihistamines should not substitute for exposure control or bronchodilators.

whySeeSpecialist: A chest physician or occupational medicine specialist should be consulted when a hemp worker develops persistent chest tightness, cough, or spirometric decline. Occupational physicians can formally document exposure history, arrange shift spirometry and workplace sampling, certify the occupational origin for workers' compensation, and liaise with employers to trigger workplace inspections. Respiratory physicians manage the COPD-spectrum airway disease that develops in Grade III cases and can offer pulmonary rehabilitation referrals. Early specialist involvement before fixed airflow limitation develops gives the best chance of halting disease progression.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a pulmonologist with subspecialty training in occupational lung disease, or an occupational medicine physician accredited in respiratory disease assessment. Ideally the clinician should have experience with byssinosis-spectrum conditions and access to serial spirometry, shift spirometry capability, and links to occupational hygienists who can perform workplace sampling. In countries with national occupational disease registries, the specialist should know how to submit a formal notification, which protects the worker's compensation rights.
preventionStrategies: Install and maintain local exhaust ventilation at all raw hemp bale-opening, hackling, and carding stations to keep inhalable hemp dust below recommended occupational exposure limits.
Use unretted (green-dried) hemp fibre where possible, or store bales in dry conditions to limit microbial colonisation and endotoxin generation.
Provide fit-tested P3 respirators as a minimum secondary protection where engineering controls alone do not achieve adequate dust reduction.
Conduct pre-placement and annual spirometry for all workers in high-dust departments to detect early lung function decline before Grade II or III disease develops.
Implement no-smoking policies in and around hemp processing facilities, given the additive lung toxicity of smoking and hemp dust.
Rotate workers to reduce individual cumulative exposure time in the highest-dust roles, particularly bale opening and hackling.
lifestyleModifications: Stop smoking: this is the highest-priority lifestyle change and the most powerful individual action to slow disease progression.
Inform your employer of symptoms as early as possible — early reporting triggers assessment and dust controls that protect both you and co-workers.
Use your respirator correctly on every shift, including during cleaning and maintenance tasks when dust resuspension is highest.
Stay well hydrated during shifts to support mucociliary clearance.
Maintain regular aerobic exercise outside work to preserve exercise capacity and respiratory muscle function.
Receive annual influenza and pneumococcal vaccinations to reduce the risk of chest infections that can trigger acute deterioration.

recommended: Adequate caloric intake to maintain healthy weight — underweight workers with chronic obstructive disease have poorer outcomes
Antioxidant-rich foods including fruits, vegetables, and nuts, which may modestly attenuate oxidative airway injury from dust exposure
Sufficient fluid intake throughout the working shift to keep airway secretions mobile
Omega-3 fatty acids from fish or plant sources, which have anti-inflammatory properties relevant to endotoxin-driven airway disease
avoid: Smoking and all tobacco products, the single most harmful combination with hemp dust exposure
Dusty or mould-contaminated food stored in the work area, which adds to total organic dust and endotoxin inhalation
Excessive alcohol, which impairs ciliary function and immune defence against chest infections

exerciseGuidelines: Regular moderate-intensity aerobic exercise — such as 30 minutes of brisk walking or cycling most days — is encouraged for all workers at risk of or diagnosed with cannabinosis. Exercise does not worsen the condition and actively counters the deconditioning that accelerates disability in chronic disease. Workers with Grade III disease and FEV1 below 50% predicted should join a supervised pulmonary rehabilitation programme rather than exercising unsupervised, as exercise prescription needs to be tailored to ventilatory limitation. During periods of acute chest tightness at work, strenuous exertion should be avoided until bronchodilator treatment has eased the airways.
dailyManagement: Record peak flow each morning before work and at the end of each shift; share the log with your respiratory physician at each visit.
Take any prescribed long-acting bronchodilator at the same time each day whether or not you feel symptomatic.
Keep a rescue salbutamol inhaler accessible at your workstation for acute chest tightness.
Report any worsening of symptoms — increased cough, more sputum, fever, or reduced exercise tolerance — promptly rather than waiting for a scheduled appointment.
Keep your employer's occupational health service informed of any spirometry results so that workplace exposure data and health surveillance are linked.
prognosis: The prognosis of cannabinosis depends heavily on the grade at diagnosis and on whether exposure is reduced or eliminated. Workers identified at Grade I, who still have fully reversible bronchoconstriction, have an excellent outcome if they are moved to dust-free work or if their workplace dust concentrations are brought below safe limits promptly; lung function typically stabilises and symptoms resolve. Grade II workers who continue to be exposed face a measurable annual FEV1 decline of approximately 30-60 mL per year — roughly double the normal age-related rate — meaning that without intervention, moderate-to-severe airflow limitation develops within a decade. Workers who reach Grade III with persistent obstruction have a prognosis equivalent to COPD: life expectancy is reduced proportionally to FEV1, and disability from breathlessness is common. Studies of hemp workers in Eastern Europe who were removed from dust exposure in Grade II show partial recovery of spirometric values over one to two years, suggesting that even at this stage, removal from exposure halts progression and allows some resolution of reversible inflammatory components. Smoking cessation at any stage provides additional benefit. Overall, cannabinosis is a preventable disease — its prognosis is determined almost entirely by how promptly the exposure is controlled rather than by the treatment applied after the fact.
recoveryTimeline: Acute Grade I symptoms — chest tightness and cough — typically resolve within hours of leaving the dusty environment and are usually gone by the following morning. After weekends or annual leave, workers report feeling entirely normal, which is precisely the diagnostic clue. Once the worker is permanently removed from hemp dust exposure at Grade I or early Grade II, spirometric improvement can be documented within three to six months, with continued slow recovery over one to two years. Grade III disease does not significantly reverse after removal from exposure but progression stops, and symptoms may stabilise or partially improve with bronchodilator therapy and pulmonary rehabilitation over months. Recovery from an acute infectious exacerbation superimposed on Grade III disease typically takes two to six weeks.
complications: Permanent COPD-equivalent airflow obstruction that persists despite removal from exposure, arising after years of uncontrolled Grade II or III cannabinosis and limiting exercise capacity throughout the remainder of the worker's life.

Acute infective exacerbations — viral and bacterial bronchitis triggering severe breathlessness and hospitalisation in workers with advanced fixed obstruction, similar in nature and frequency to COPD exacerbations.
Respiratory failure requiring supplemental oxygen in end-stage Grade III disease, when resting PaO2 falls below 8 kPa despite maximal bronchodilator therapy.
Cor pulmonale — right heart failure secondary to chronic hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction in workers with severe long-standing obstruction and hypoxaemia.
Superimposed hypersensitivity pneumonitis from mould antigens on poorly stored hemp bales, presenting with fever, malaise, and diffuse alveolar infiltrates that can be confused with pneumonia.
Psychological impact of occupational disability including anxiety, depression, and loss of livelihood, particularly in workers whose only job skills are in hemp processing industries.

supportResources.name: NIOSH — Byssinosis (Cotton Dust Lung Disease)
supportResources.description: NIOSH overview of organic dust diseases relevant to hemp and other vegetable-fibre workers, with guidance on exposure assessment and control.

supportResources.name: ILO — Encyclopaedia of Occupational Health and Safety: Byssinosis
supportResources.description: International Labour Organization encyclopaedia chapter on byssinosis-spectrum conditions including cannabinosis, covering epidemiology, prevention, and workers' rights.

sources.title: Rylander R. Endotoxin and occupational airway disease. Curr Opin Allergy Clin Immunol. 2006
Zuskin E et al. Respiratory function in hemp workers. Br J Ind Med. 1990
Fishwick D et al. Respiratory symptoms, lung function and dust exposure in flour, hemp and jute workers. Am J Respir Crit Care Med. 1996
WHO — Byssinosis: Epidemiology and mechanisms. World Health Organization
NIOSH — NIOSH Pocket Guide: Cotton dust and related vegetable fibre dusts
Hendrick DJ. Occupational and environmental asthma. In: Fishman's Pulmonary Diseases and Disorders, 5th edition. McGraw-Hill, 2015

confusedWithConditions.name: Byssinosis (cotton dust lung)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Byssinosis is caused by cotton dust rather than hemp dust but shares the identical Monday morning chest tightness pattern and endotoxin mechanism; ICD-10 separates them as J66.0 (cotton) versus J66.2 (hemp), but clinical features overlap almost completely.

confusedWithConditions.name: Asthma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Non-occupational asthma lacks the characteristic work-related weekly pattern; in cannabinosis, symptoms are consistently worst on Monday after a break and improve during time away from the hemp mill.

confusedWithConditions.name: COPD
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Chronic Grade III cannabinosis produces fixed airflow obstruction indistinguishable from COPD on spirometry alone; the key differentiator is a history of hemp mill employment and the progressive course from Grade I through III, as opposed to COPD developing purely from tobacco exposure.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hypersensitivity pneumonitis from organic dust
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hypersensitivity pneumonitis from mouldy hemp causes a six-hour delayed febrile 'flu-like' reaction with diffuse lung infiltrates on imaging, whereas cannabinosis presents with immediate bronchoconstriction without fever or radiographic infiltrates.

relatedConditions.name: Byssinosis
relatedConditions.relevance: The closest related condition — cotton dust byssinosis shares the same endotoxin mechanism, Monday chest pattern, and grading system as cannabinosis and is classified in the same ICD-10 J66 category.

relatedConditions.name: Flax-dressers' disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Linen (flax) processing produces similar organic dust and endotoxin disease to hemp processing; the conditions are clinically indistinguishable and grouped together in occupational lung disease classifications.

relatedConditions.name: Airway disease from other organic dusts
relatedConditions.relevance: A broader category in ICD-10 J66 that includes dust from jute, sisal, and other plant fibres causing the same pattern of occupational airway disease.

relatedConditions.name: Occupational asthma
relatedConditions.relevance: True sensitisation-type occupational asthma from hemp allergens co-occurs in some workers and requires a different management approach (full removal from exposure) compared with non-immunological cannabinosis.

faqs.question: What is cannabinosis?
faqs.answer: Cannabinosis is an occupational lung disease caused by inhaling hemp dust during the processing of Cannabis sativa fibres. It produces chest tightness, cough, and airway obstruction, most noticeably at the start of each working week after a break from the hemp mill.

faqs.question: Is cannabinosis the same as getting high from cannabis?
faqs.answer: No. Cannabinosis is a dust-related lung disease caused by inhaling hemp fibre particles and endotoxin in textile or rope-making factories. It has nothing to do with cannabis intoxication, THC, or recreational drug use.

faqs.question: What causes the Monday morning chest tightness in hemp workers?
faqs.answer: After a weekend away from the hemp mill, the airways partly recover their sensitivity to endotoxin. On returning Monday morning, inhalation of hemp dust triggers an acute immune response producing bronchoconstriction. The reaction lessens later in the week as the airways temporarily adapt.

faqs.question: Can cannabinosis cause permanent lung damage?
faqs.answer: Yes. Workers who remain in high-dust hemp jobs for 10 or more years can develop permanent airflow obstruction similar to COPD that does not fully reverse even after leaving the industry. Early detection and dust control prevent this outcome.

faqs.question: How is cannabinosis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis combines an occupational history of hemp mill work, a work-related Monday symptom pattern, and serial peak flow measurements at work versus at home. Pre- and post-shift spirometry showing a fall in FEV1 across the shift confirms acute bronchoconstriction.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for cannabinosis?
faqs.answer: The primary treatment is reducing hemp dust exposure through ventilation controls or job change. Inhaled bronchodilators — short-acting for acute relief and long-acting for persistent obstruction — manage symptoms. Smoking cessation is the single most important lifestyle intervention.

faqs.question: Does cannabinosis improve if I leave the hemp factory?
faqs.answer: At Grade I, symptoms and spirometry normalise within months of leaving. At Grade II, partial improvement occurs over one to two years. Grade III disease (permanent obstruction) stops progressing after removal but does not fully reverse. Early removal gives the best outcome.

faqs.question: Should I wear a mask in a hemp mill?
faqs.answer: Yes. A correctly fitted P3 half-face respirator or powered air-purifying respirator reduces endotoxin inhalation by tenfold or more and is mandatory when engineering dust controls cannot achieve safe airborne dust levels. Respirators must be fit-tested annually.

faqs.question: Does smoking make cannabinosis worse?
faqs.answer: Smoking accelerates annual lung function decline and greatly increases the risk of progressing from reversible to permanent disease. Quitting smoking is the highest-priority action a hemp worker with any respiratory symptoms can take.

faqs.question: Is cannabinosis a notifiable occupational disease?
faqs.answer: In many countries including the UK, cannabinosis and byssinosis-spectrum diseases are notifiable occupational diseases, meaning clinicians must report confirmed cases to national authorities. This triggers workplace inspection and entitles the worker to compensation review.

faqs.question: What spirometry pattern does cannabinosis produce?
faqs.answer: Acute cannabinosis shows a reversible obstructive pattern — reduced FEV1/FVC ratio with bronchodilator response. Chronic Grade III disease produces fixed obstruction identical to COPD spirometry.

faqs.question: Can hemp workers get compensation for cannabinosis?
faqs.answer: In most developed countries, cannabinosis and other organic-dust lung diseases are prescribed industrial diseases that entitle workers to disability benefit and compensation if exposure can be documented. An occupational physician can help prepare the medical evidence.

faqs.question: How is cannabinosis different from hypersensitivity pneumonitis?
faqs.answer: Hypersensitivity pneumonitis from mouldy hemp causes fever, flu-like symptoms, and lung infiltrates on a chest X-ray starting four to eight hours after exposure. Cannabinosis causes immediate bronchoconstriction without fever or radiographic changes.

faqs.question: What dust level is safe in a hemp mill?
faqs.answer: Occupational exposure limits vary by country but are generally set at around 0.5-1 mg/m³ for total inhalable cotton and hemp dust. More importantly, endotoxin levels below 90 EU/m³ are associated with lower rates of lung function decline in organic dust studies.

faqs.question: Can women develop cannabinosis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cannabinosis affects both male and female hemp workers equally; gender is not a significant risk factor. The main determinants are dust exposure level and duration, smoking status, and pre-existing airway hyperresponsiveness.

faqs.question: Is there a vaccine or preventive medication for cannabinosis?
faqs.answer: There is no vaccine. Pre-shift inhaled salbutamol blunts the acute bronchoconstriction of individual shifts but does not prevent cumulative lung damage. The only effective prevention is reducing hemp dust inhalation through engineering controls and respiratory protective equipment.

faqs.question: Can cannabinosis cause lung cancer?
faqs.answer: Cannabinosis itself is not associated with lung cancer. However, some hemp workers are also exposed to other occupational carcinogens, and smoking — common among hemp mill workers — is a major lung cancer risk. Persistent cough and haemoptysis should always be investigated to exclude malignancy.

faqs.question: What should I do if I notice chest tightness every Monday at work?
faqs.answer: Tell your employer's occupational health service and your own doctor promptly. Request pre- and post-shift spirometry and serial peak flow monitoring. Early reporting triggers dust assessment and may prevent permanent lung damage.

faqs.question: Is cannabinosis the same as byssinosis?
faqs.answer: They are closely related but distinct ICD-10 conditions: byssinosis (J66.0) is caused by cotton dust, cannabinosis (J66.2) by hemp dust. Both share the Monday chest pattern and endotoxin mechanism, and are treated identically.

faqs.question: What happens to lung function over a career in hemp milling without protection?
faqs.answer: Unprotected hemp mill workers lose an estimated 30-60 mL of FEV1 per year above normal ageing, meaning a 20-year career can cost 600-1200 mL of lung function — equivalent to developing moderate COPD purely from occupational dust exposure.

metaDescription: Cannabinosis is hemp dust lung disease causing Monday chest tightness and airway obstruction in mill workers. Learn symptoms, diagnosis and treatment.
